name: Lepidozamia
description: [FILL IN] Lepidozamia is a genus of cycads endemic to eastern Australia, comprising only two species. They are among the tallest cycads in the world, with trunks reaching up to 7 metres in some individuals. Lepidozamia species typically grow in moist subtropical and tropical rainforests along Australia's east coast.
etymology: [FILL IN] From the Greek 'lepis' (scale) and 'Zamia', referring to the scaled appearance of the cones.

conservation_status.notes: [FILL IN] Both species are listed under Australian legislation; L. hopei is listed as Vulnerable.

distribution.habitat: [FILL IN] Moist subtropical and tropical rainforest margins; gullies and creek banks.

morphology.growth_form: [FILL IN] Tall, arborescent cycad; one of the world's tallest cycad genera.
morphology.height_m: [FILL IN] Up to 7 m in L. hopei.
morphology.stem: [FILL IN] Robust columnar trunk covered in persistent leaf-base scars.
morphology.leaves: [FILL IN] Large, glossy, pinnately compound fronds; leaflets lanceolate, dark green and shiny.
morphology.reproductive_structures: [FILL IN] Dioecious; female cones very large and barrel-shaped; male cones elongated cylindrical.
morphology.seeds_or_spores: [FILL IN] Large seeds with bright red sarcotesta.
morphology.roots: [FILL IN] Coralloid roots with nitrogen-fixing cyanobacteria.

ecology.seed_dispersal: [FILL IN] Large seeds dispersed by cassowaries, possums, and other vertebrates.
ecology.symbioses: [FILL IN] Cyanobacterial nitrogen fixation in coralloid roots.

uses.cultural: [FILL IN] Seeds used as food by Aboriginal Australians after processing to remove toxins.
uses.food: [FILL IN] Seeds prepared and eaten by Aboriginal peoples of Queensland.
uses.horticultural: [FILL IN] Cultivated as ornamental in tropical and subtropical gardens.

cultivation.soil: [FILL IN] Rich, well-drained soil with organic matter.
cultivation.light: [FILL IN] Partial shade to full sun.
cultivation.watering: [FILL IN] Regular watering; prefers moist conditions.
cultivation.notes: [FILL IN] Slow growing; CITES Appendix II listed.

notable_species.notes: [FILL IN] Tallest cycad species; endemic to north Queensland.
[FILL IN] More southerly distribution; named for Count Peroffsky.
